findings.title: methodHistory sidecar is gitignored and ephemeral on runners, so per-stock per-method history is wiped every workflow run
findings.description: Tag 134 Phase 5.2 moved methodHistory from the committed alert-state.json into external-data/method-history-state.json to fight the 25 MB per-push bloat. That sidecar is listed in .gitignore (lines 29-31: 'external-data/method-history-state.json'). GitHub Actions runners are provisioned fresh per run via actions/checkout@v4; nothing in the workflow restores this sidecar (no actions/cache, no upload-artifact/download-artifact, no external storage). _loadMethodHistory() therefore returns {} on every run, and saveState() writes a sidecar that is discarded at job tear-down. Trend.computeTrend() needs >=3 datapoints to emit anything other than 'n/a', and appendHistory caps at HISTORY_WINDOW=8, so trend direction is now permanently stuck at 'n/a' for the production pipeline.
findings.mechanism: loadState() at line 81 calls _loadMethodHistory(), which fs.existsSync(HISTORY_SIDECAR) returns false on a fresh runner. inlineHistory at line 80 only matches when alert-state.json still has methodHistory inline (true on the first migration run only). Hence methodHistory := {}. The detect-changes loop at lines 220-229 reads state.methodHistory[ticker] (undefined -> {}), appends one entry per method, and writes that single-entry-per-method state back to the gitignored sidecar. Next run: same thing. History never accumulates.
findings.reproducer: On a clean clone (no external-data/method-history-state.json), run: node detect-changes.js --snapshots ./snapshots --state ./alert-state.json. Then inspect external-data/method-history-state.json -> every history array has length 1. Run again: still length 1, because the sidecar from the first run was overwritten with the second run's single-entry state (which only saw {} as prior history because of cold start). On GitHub Actions every run is a cold start.
findings.impact: Trend.appendHistory / computeTrend cannot produce a meaningful 'improving|stable|deteriorating' signal in production. Any downstream consumer of method-history trend data is silently degraded to no-op. Method-Drift / Methodology Report dashboards relying on multi-week per-stock-per-method trends show 'n/a' forever.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) commit the sidecar (remove from .gitignore — but this re-introduces the 25 MB bloat that motivated the split), or (b) add an actions/cache@v4 step keyed by repository (not by sha) before detect-changes and an upload step after, or (c) move the sidecar back into git but cap each per-stock-per-method array to HISTORY_WINDOW=8 entries (which Trend.appendHistory already does) so the file stays at ~3 MB rather than 25 MB. (c) is the cleanest fix — the bloat-vs-correctness tradeoff that prompted the move was probably a misdiagnosis of the actual size after the WINDOW=8 cap.
findings.compound_hint: Pairs with F-SM-002 (loadState migration path silently swallows the symptom on the very first post-deploy run, then 'works' from then on).

findings.title: _saveMethodHistory writes the sidecar without tmp+rename, so a SIGKILL/disk-full mid-write yields an unparseable sidecar and silent history rewind
findings.description: saveState() (lines 96-107) uses an atomic tmp+rename for the committed alert-state.json. _saveMethodHistory() (lines 57-62) does not — it writes directly to the final HISTORY_SIDECAR path. If the runner is killed (timeout-minutes: 180 hit, OOM, etc.) mid-fs.writeFileSync, the sidecar is left truncated. On the next run, _loadMethodHistory() catches the JSON.parse exception (line 51) and returns {} with only a WARN log. The committed alert-state.json by contrast WAS written atomically and reflects the new lastRun + methodState. Result: methodState is at run N+1, methodHistory regresses to empty.
findings.mechanism: fs.writeFileSync is not atomic on any OS. POSIX fsync isn't called, and the rename trick is missing. On Windows specifically (Karl runs locally on win32 per env block), antivirus scanning can also temporarily lock the partially-written file and observe a truncated state.
findings.reproducer: 1. Populate the sidecar with valid data. 2. Modify _saveMethodHistory to throw after writeFileSync starts but before it completes (e.g., wrap in a setTimeout(process.exit, ...) racing the write). 3. Restart the script. _loadMethodHistory logs 'history sidecar unparseable, treating as fresh' and returns {}.
findings.impact: Silent rewind of the entire methodHistory state. Combined with F-SM-001 this means the only thing that can produce trend signals — a cached sidecar on a stable runner — can be wiped by any abnormal termination.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the saveState pattern: write to HISTORY_SIDECAR + '.tmp.' + process.pid, then fs.renameSync. Optionally fs.fsyncSync the fd before rename for crash-safety.

findings.title: Workflow YAML strips methodHistory key with a non-atomic writeFileSync in-place, leaving alert-state.json corrupt if the step is killed mid-write
findings.description: The 'Truncate alert-state if too large' step inlines: JSON.parse(readFileSync('alert-state.json')) -> delete s.methodHistory -> fs.writeFileSync('alert-state.json', JSON.stringify(s)). This is a destructive in-place rewrite. If the step is interrupted (Actions cancellation, OOM during JSON.stringify on the 11 MB file, etc.), alert-state.json is left truncated. The next workflow run's detect-changes loadState() will catch the parse error and reset to fallback, silently losing the entire methodState (currently tracking ~12,000 tickers × ~30 methods of pass/fail/lastChanged state).
findings.mechanism: No tmp+rename. The committed file on origin/main is the input AND the output. There is no rollback path. Note the rebase-with-strategy-option-theirs at line 270 means a corrupted file pushed to main will OVERRIDE any concurrent fix attempt.
findings.reproducer: Manually kill the workflow during the Truncate step. On the next manual run, observe '[WARN] state-file unparseable, treating as fresh: ...' in detect-changes logs and a fully empty methodState section.
findings.impact: Single corruption event wipes all METHOD_PASS_LOST/METHOD_PASS_GAINED baselines — every method/ticker pair becomes 'first-seen' for one full run, generating a wave of false PASS_GAINED INFO events the run AFTER recovery (because previous run wrote fresh state with today's pass status as the baseline).
findings.suggested_fix: Write to alert-state.json.tmp then renameSync. Or, better, push this logic into detect-changes.js's saveState (where the atomic tmp+rename already exists) and remove the YAML inline node -e entirely.

findings.title: snapshot-picks.js writes YYYY-MM-DD.json and latest.json non-atomically and not in lockstep, allowing latest.json to point to a different vintage than the dated file
findings.description: Lines 195 and 197 perform two independent fs.writeFileSync calls. Between them the process can be killed, leaving the dated file written but latest.json still pointing to yesterday's vintage (or, on a re-run, today's dated file being valid while latest.json being a partial truncation). picks-regression-check, walk-forward-perf, methodology-report, and pick-diff all read picks-history; some read latest.json, some scan dated files. A mismatch between them silently mis-attributes pick continuity across runs.
findings.mechanism: No tmp+rename. No transactional 'two-phase commit' of (dated, latest). The pipeline-health-check step (line 208) is also non-atomic on its own file but lower severity since it is transient/gitignored.
findings.reproducer: Add a process.exit(0) between lines 195 and 197. Inspect latest.json — it now references yesterday's pick set; the dated file shows today's set.
findings.impact: _buildFirstSeenMap (lines 100-120) sorts dated files and walks them to compute firstSeenAt/weeksOnList. If today's dated file is written but latest.json wasn't, downstream consumers using latest.json undercount 'weeksOnList' for tickers that first appeared today. Walk-forward-perf reads dated vintages, so its prices×picks join is fine, but the dashboard / pick-diff that reads latest.json shows stale data — silent staleness with no error.
findings.suggested_fix: Write to a temp path then rename for both files. Alternatively, write the dated file first, fsync, then atomically symlink latest.json -> dated file (on Linux runners; on Windows fall back to copy).

findings.title: snapshot-methods-history.js writes vintage file non-atomically; a partial write produces an unparseable vintage that breaks backtests
findings.description: fs.writeFileSync(outFile, JSON.stringify(data, null, 2)) writes the 14 MB vintage in place. If the process is killed mid-stringify (it has been observed at OOM thresholds — see scripts/archive-old-snapshots.js noting 14 MB per file), the YYYY-MM-DD.json is truncated mid-JSON. scripts/method-effectiveness.js, scripts/walk-forward-perf.js, backtest-from-history.js, and backtest-10-weeks.js all readFile + JSON.parse these vintages; one corrupt vintage will throw, often silently (catch returns empty in walk-forward).
findings.mechanism: No tmp+rename. Note that the comparable structural step archive-old-snapshots.js (lines 78-82) DOES use append-mode logic but still writeFileSync to the final path — same flaw for the archive.
findings.reproducer: Inject a SIGKILL between JSON.stringify start and writeFileSync flush. Verify methods-history/YYYY-MM-DD.json is invalid JSON. Run backtest-from-history.js — it throws or silently skips the vintage depending on the consumer.
findings.impact: Backtest series gets a gap. Walk-forward-perf has its own try/catch and emits a warning, but methodology-report consumes its output and the gap propagates as 'no signal'.
findings.suggested_fix: Use tmp+rename. Same pattern as detect-changes.js saveState. This is a one-line change.

findings.title: alert-state.json methodState grows linearly with universe × methods with no pruning; currently 11 MB and approaching the 50 MB GitHub soft-warn threshold
findings.description: saveState writes the full methodState ({ticker: {methodId: {value,pass,lastChanged}}}) every run. Universe target per workflow comments is 13,000 tickers; ~30 methods registered in methods/index.js. That's ~390K entries × ~80 bytes = ~31 MB after JSON minification. Today the file is already 11 MB (verified via stat). There is NO pruning of dead/delisted tickers — once a ticker enters methodState it stays forever. scripts/prune-watchlist.js removes from watchlist.json but does not touch alert-state.methodState. Tag 142 added prune-watchlist but the cleanup never reached the state file.
findings.mechanism: loadState reads the full file (line 69), keeps every ticker key, and saveState writes it back. detectMethodDiffs only mutates state.methodState[ticker] for tickers present in the current run's snapshots. Tickers absent from this run's snapshots/ folder still exist in newState? Looking at line 197: newState.methodState = {} starts fresh. Then for each file in snapshots/, newState.methodState[ticker] = tickerNewState (line 218). Tickers not in snapshots/ are DROPPED. Good — there IS implicit pruning. BUT a single failed Yahoo Pull (which the workflow tolerates via continue-on-error: true on line 81) reduces snapshots/ to whatever partial set landed before timeout, and that's what becomes the new alert-state.json. Next run all the missing tickers are 'first-seen' again.
findings.reproducer: Look at the Verify Pull Coverage step (line 84-106): it allows as little as max(1800, 40%×total) snapshots. If a pull lands 5000 of 12000 expected tickers, alert-state.methodState shrinks from 12000 entries to 5000. The OTHER 7000 tickers' state — including their lastChanged dates which preserve when a pass-flip last happened — is GONE.
findings.impact: Combined with F-SM-001, the loss of lastChanged dates means METHOD_PASS_LOST/GAINED detection compares against {} for those 7000 tickers next run, generating a wave of spurious 'first detection' classifications (which the code currently suppresses — see line 124's wasComputable && isComputable guard — so events are suppressed, but the state is still rewritten with today's value as if it were the baseline).
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) preserve unseen tickers' state across runs by initialising newState.methodState = state.methodState and only overwriting present tickers, plus a separate TTL-based prune (e.g., drop tickers whose lastChanged is older than 90 days), or (b) accept the current 'snapshots are source of truth' model but document and add an alert when day-over-day ticker count drops >10%.
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with the picks-regression-check at workflow line 142 (which IS designed to catch this kind of shrinkage, but only at the picks-history layer).

findings.title: loadState migration path silently picks sidecar over inline even when sidecar is empty {} and inline has accumulated data
findings.description: Line 82: const methodHistory = Object.keys(sidecarHistory).length > 0 ? sidecarHistory : (inlineHistory || {}). Intent: 'if sidecar has data, use it; otherwise fall back to inline.' The check is on sidecarHistory keys (i.e., number of tickers), not on whether the sidecar FILE existed. If a previous run wrote an empty sidecar (e.g., after the F-SM-001 wipe), Object.keys(sidecarHistory).length === 0, so inlineHistory wins — but the workflow YAML at line 256 has already deleted s.methodHistory before commit. So inlineHistory is also null. methodHistory falls back to {}. The migration path is correct only on the very first post-deploy run; after that, both branches are typically empty.
findings.mechanism: Three-state matrix: (sidecar empty | inline empty) -> {}; (sidecar empty | inline present) -> inline (the intended migration handoff, fires once); (sidecar present | inline anything) -> sidecar. Today the workflow design forces inline-present to never occur after the migration commit, and F-SM-001 forces sidecar-present to never occur on cold runners. So the branch executed in production is always the {} fallback.
findings.reproducer: Inspect the post-Tag-134-Phase-5.2 alert-state.json on origin/main — methodHistory key is absent (workflow strips it). Inspect external-data/ on a fresh runner — sidecar file doesn't exist (gitignored). Read line 82: methodHistory = {} every time.
findings.impact: Same as F-SM-001: trend signals can't accumulate. This finding is the 'why' from a state-machine perspective.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the migration path observable: log when sidecarHistory is empty AND inlineHistory is present (one-shot migration log) vs. empty-empty (cold-start warning). Today both look identical in logs.

findings.title: saveState writes committed file BEFORE sidecar; if sidecar write fails the two stores skew and history rewinds invisibly
findings.description: saveState advances the committed file (line 104-105) and THEN attempts the sidecar (line 106). _saveMethodHistory has a try/catch that swallows failures with only a WARN (line 61). Net effect: lastRun and methodState are committed and pushed, methodHistory is silently stale. Next run loadState sees the new lastRun, treats the now-stale sidecar as authoritative, and tomorrow's METHOD_PASS_LOST diff is computed against an inconsistent snapshot — methodState says 'as of today's pass status' while methodHistory's last appended entry is yesterday's value.
findings.mechanism: Write-order asymmetry: critical state advances before side-state. The try/catch downgrades a failure to a warning that nothing reads (Discord webhook isn't invoked for state-save failures).
findings.reproducer: Force the sidecar dir to be read-only (chmod 0500). Run detect-changes. Observe alert-state.json updated, sidecar untouched, exit code 0.
findings.impact: Trend computation on day N+1 sees yesterday's history entry as 'previous' but uses today's methodState/value when checking diff continuity. Skew between methodState lastChanged and methodHistory[i].date can confuse downstream walk-forward-perf and method-effectiveness.
findings.suggested_fix: Write sidecar first; if it fails, abort before advancing the committed file. Or write both to tmp paths, fsync both, then rename both. Two-phase commit semantics.

findings.title: Tmp filename suffix is process-pid only, so a stale .tmp file from a killed prior run can collide on PID reuse on the same runner
findings.description: Line 103: const tmp = statePath + '.tmp.' + process.pid. GitHub Actions runners are ephemeral so PID reuse across runs is unlikely (fresh kernel), but on a single runner where multiple node processes coexist (e.g., if a developer runs the script locally while a different script also writes to alert-state) PID collisions are possible. More importantly: there is no cleanup of stale .tmp.* files on the next run. If saveState is killed AFTER writeFileSync(tmp) but BEFORE renameSync, the tmp file persists in the repo. git add -A (workflow line 263) commits it. Over time alert-state.json.tmp.12345, alert-state.json.tmp.67890, ... accumulate in the repo.
findings.mechanism: No try/finally cleanup of tmp on rename failure. No glob-and-rm of .tmp.* at start of saveState. git add -A indiscriminately adds anything matching.
findings.reproducer: kill -9 the node process between writeFileSync(tmp) and renameSync(tmp,...). After the next commit, inspect repo: alert-state.json.tmp.<pid> is now tracked in git.
findings.impact: Repo bloat (small per occurrence but additive); accidental commit of partial/corrupt state could confuse downstream tooling that does fs.readdirSync to find state files.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `try { fs.readdirSync(path.dirname(statePath)).filter(f => f.startsWith(path.basename(statePath)+'.tmp.')).forEach(f => fs.unlinkSync(path.join(path.dirname(statePath), f))); } catch(e){}` at start of saveState. Or use crypto.randomBytes(8).toString('hex') instead of process.pid and wrap in try/finally.

findings.title: fieldCoverage.history grows by 1 entry per run capped at 14 but the cap is silently re-applied on every load, hiding history-window changes from observability
findings.description: FieldCoverage.updateHistory takes the existing array and slices to HISTORY_WINDOW=14. detect-changes.js loadState reads parsed.fieldCoverage.history as-is (line 89) — no slice. So if HISTORY_WINDOW is later changed (e.g., to 30), the old 14-entry history persists and is extended to 30 over the next 16 runs. Fine. But if HISTORY_WINDOW is REDUCED (e.g., to 7), updateHistory slices to 7 on the next run and the older 7 entries are silently dropped without log. computeBaseline uses 'useEntries = safe.slice(0, -1)' which is the prior history; reducing window mid-flight changes baseline calculation invisibly.
findings.mechanism: Sliding-window is fine, but window-resize is a silent migration.
findings.reproducer: Set HISTORY_WINDOW = 7 in field-coverage.js. Next detect-changes run drops 7 entries with no log.
findings.impact: Low; mostly observability. fieldCoverage is small enough that it doesn't bloat the file. Drift detection still works.
findings.suggested_fix: Log when updateHistory truncates more than 1 entry (i.e., window resized).

findings.title: Concurrent workflow runs can interleave reads/writes of alert-state.json via the rebase-with-theirs strategy, last-writer-wins silently overwrites the other run's state
findings.description: The 'concurrency: group: main-push, cancel-in-progress: false' guarantee only serialises pushes for the daily-pull workflow itself. But the comment at line 13 explicitly mentions a second workflow (monthly-sec-xbrl) that also pushes via retry-rebase. Whichever run finishes second wins because line 270 uses `git pull --rebase --autostash --strategy-option theirs origin main`. 'theirs' (in a rebase context) means OURS (the local working copy) is preferred over origin/main during conflict resolution — so the local copy's alert-state.json wholesale REPLACES the version that the other workflow may have just pushed, regardless of which content is newer.
findings.mechanism: Rebase semantics flip ours/theirs: during `git pull --rebase`, 'theirs' refers to the upstream commits being replayed onto OUR branch, so each conflicting upstream commit is discarded in favor of our local change. If workflow A pushes alert-state changes at T0, workflow B (started before A pushed) reads the pre-A state at T-1, computes diffs, and pushes at T+1 — B's pull --rebase --strategy-option theirs DISCARDS A's commit and replaces it with B's. A's state mutation is lost.
findings.reproducer: Trigger daily-pull and a second workflow that writes alert-state.json simultaneously. Second run pushes; its alert-state contains only B's mutations; A's contributions vanish.
findings.impact: Combined with F-SM-006 (no merging logic in saveState), overlapping runs cause shared-state corruption. Today there's only one workflow that touches alert-state, but the architecture is fragile.
findings.suggested_fix: Use --strategy-option ours (force OUR alert-state to lose to upstream) and re-apply the local mutation via a deterministic loadState->mutate->saveState in a retry loop. Or move alert-state to a non-versioned store (S3 / artifact cache with a lock).
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with F-SM-003 (corrupt state from non-atomic strip).

findings.title: detectMethodDiffs preserves lastChanged across non-flip transitions but uses 'today' instead of lastChanged for the first observation, masking 'computable for the first time' as a fresh change
findings.description: When wasComputable=false (no prev) and isComputable=true (just got data), the else branch on line 142 sets newState[methodId] = { value, pass, lastChanged: prev && prev.lastChanged ? prev.lastChanged : today }. Since prev is undefined here, lastChanged := today. That's a sensible default, BUT no event is emitted (the if-guard on line 124 requires wasComputable && isComputable). So when a method becomes computable for the first time, the state silently transitions to today's lastChanged with no signal — a flip from 'no data' to 'PASS' is invisible.
findings.mechanism: Three transition cases: (incomputable -> computable PASS) silent; (incomputable -> computable FAIL) silent; (computable -> computable, flip) event. The 'first-seen' case is intentionally suppressed (comment line 151: 'skip per default — alert-noise') but lastChanged is still set to today, making subsequent trend analysis treat day-N as the start. If a method was actually passing for years before Yahoo data started flowing for that ticker, the system has no way to distinguish 'new pass' from 'newly observable old pass'.
findings.reproducer: First-time evaluation of a brand-new ticker. prevMethods is {}. wasComputable=false. Method evaluates to {computable:true, pass:true}. Line 144-148 sets lastChanged=today. No event. Trend computed from a single datapoint of today.
findings.impact: Trend interpretation skews — backtests will treat day-of-first-observation as the start of a pass streak when it might just be the start of data availability. Magnitude depends on universe churn rate.
findings.suggested_fix: Distinguish 'incomputable->computable' from 'flip' explicitly. Optionally emit a low-severity FIRST_SEEN_COMPUTABLE event behind a flag, or set lastChanged=null in this case so consumers know.

findings.title: archive-old-snapshots writes NDJSON archive then unlinks originals without verifying the archive write succeeded
findings.description: Line 78-82 writes the NDJSON archive (append or full write). Line 84-86 then unlinks the originals. If writeFileSync/appendFileSync silently truncates (disk full mid-write), the unlinkSync still fires. Net effect: source files deleted, archive incomplete. There's no fs.statSync verification that the new lines actually landed.
findings.mechanism: writeFileSync's error path throws — caught by main()'s outer try/catch at line 132 which exits 0 (continue-on-error: true in workflow). On disk-full it would throw before unlink, so this is mostly safe. But appendFileSync on an existing archive can partial-write without throwing (e.g., on signal-interrupted writes).
findings.reproducer: Fill disk to 99% so append succeeds for first ticker but fails midway. Originals get unlinked because the try/catch wraps the WHOLE month loop, not individual files.
findings.impact: Permanent data loss in the rare disk-full case. Archive is gitignored (line 25-27 of .gitignore) so there's no remediation path.
findings.suggested_fix: fs.statSync the archive before vs after, verify size increased by expected amount, then unlink. Or write archive to tmp + rename, then unlink originals.

findings.title: saveState in detect-changes.js does not preserve methodState entries for tickers absent from this run's snapshots, causing partial-pull silent state-loss
findings.description: main() at line 197 initialises newState.methodState = {} fresh, then loops only over files in snapshots/. Tickers absent from snapshots (e.g., because Yahoo Pull timed out before reaching them) are NOT copied from state.methodState into newState.methodState. saveState writes newState, so missing tickers are PURGED from alert-state.json. On the next run those tickers reappear with prevMethods = {} (line 216), classifying every method as first-seen (no event emitted per F-SM-012 logic, but lastChanged is reset to today). The lastChanged loss makes 'how long has this ticker been in PASS' analytics permanently inaccurate.
findings.mechanism: Quality gate at workflow line 89-106 allows down to 1800 snapshots. With a 12000-ticker universe that's a 10000-ticker state-loss in a single run. The workflow accepts this as 'partial success' rather than failing the whole run.
findings.reproducer: Drop half the snapshots/ files before running detect-changes (simulate partial pull). saveState writes alert-state.json with only the surviving half's methodState. Restore the snapshots and rerun — all the previously-purged tickers are 'first-seen' again.
findings.impact: lastChanged dates corrupted en masse. METHOD_PASS_LOST detection still works on the next run for surviving tickers, but the universe baseline becomes inconsistent (some tickers' lastChanged dates reflect 2026-05-07, others 2026-05-13 due to recent purge-recreate cycles).
findings.suggested_fix: Initialise newState.methodState = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(state.methodState)) (deep clone of prior state), then overwrite present tickers. Or initialise = state.methodState directly (mutation-by-reference is fine since saveState writes synchronously after the loop). Same fix applies conceptually to newState.methodHistory at line 197.
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with F-SM-006 (no TTL prune) — without proper preserve+prune logic, the state file either bloats or loses data depending on workflow conditions.

files_skipped: C:\Users\Karlr\OneDrive\Dokumente\GitHub\screener-data\alert-state.json (full contents — 11 MB / 5.5M tokens, exceeded Read limits; inspected via head and stat instead)
